groups.text: It is currently 26 degrees and raining in Berlin-Tempelhof.
With the morning weather set, here is the news.

Ford is rehiring 350 veteran engineers, some of whom are former employees, after artificial intelligence and automated systems failed to deliver required quality levels. Chief operating officer Kumar Galhotra says the company relied too heavily on automated quality systems with disappointing results. These specialists, referred to as gray beard engineers, now hunt for failure points before parts reach the plant floor and help train younger staff to reprogram AI tools. CEO Jim Farley notes this shift has lowered warranty and recall costs by hundreds of millions of dollars. Separately, Sina has released VibeThinker-3B, a small language model with three billion parameters that matches much larger models like DeepSeek V3.2 and Kimi K2.5 on math and coding benchmarks. While the model uses multi-stage post-training to achieve this reasoning, researchers found it still falls well behind larger models on tasks requiring broad factual knowledge.

Samsung and SK Hynix are investing 590 billion dollars into new chip factories and packaging centers to meet surging demand from AI data centers. Backed by the South Korean government, the two companies control nearly 80 percent of the global market for high-bandwidth memory chips. Jefferies Equity Research warns that memory prices could climb up to 50 percent per quarter through 2027, a trend already forcing Apple to raise prices on Macs and MacBooks. Relief may not arrive until new capacity comes online in 2028. Separately, prosecutors in Los Angeles used ChatGPT logs as evidence against Jonathan Rinderknecht, who faced arson charges for a deadly New Year's Day wildfire in 2025. Evidence included screen recordings of the defendant asking the chatbot if someone could be blamed for a fire and prompts asking why he was so angry all the time.

Western AI labs are facing a pricing stress test as companies like Coinbase shift to cheaper Chinese models. CEO Brian Armstrong says his company now uses models such as GLM 5.2 and Kimi 2.7, allowing Coinbase to cut its AI spending in half even while token usage increases. To optimize costs, the company employs an automatic routing system that selects models based on price and task, while improved caching has pushed hit rates from 5 to 60 percent. This trend extends beyond Coinbase, with Snowflake testing Chinese alternatives and the startup Lindy moving to Deepseek v4. While software costs shift, hardware demand remains high. Wall Street investors are now eyeing memory chip maker Micron as a primary AI winner. Driven by a shortage of High-Bandwidth Memory for AI servers, Micron's stock has soared over 236 percent in the past month, briefly pushing its market valuation near that of Meta and Tesla.

AI systems are currently limited by their role as reactive chatbots, but researchers argue they cannot become reliable coworkers until they stop simply generating answers and start finishing entire tasks. A new survey paper from Tencent's Youtu Lab and several Chinese universities maps this evolution toward the "digital colleague," shifting the focus from how a model produces an answer to how it turns intent into finished work within persistent environments. This transition requires moving beyond fast, one-pass text generation toward delegated task execution using reusable skills. While previous models relied on stored language patterns to write answers in a single pass, newer thinking LLMs—such as OpenAI's o1 and Deepseek-R1—invest more compute during inference to explore solution paths and correct errors before providing a final response. The goal is to move the technology through five stages of evolution, ultimately transforming AI from a basic chatbot into an autonomous colleague capable of independent execution.

Most current AI models struggle to manage long-term strategic goals, a gap highlighted by researchers at Princeton University. They developed CEO-Bench, a simulation where AI agents attempt to run a fictional software company for 500 simulated days. The results show that most models go broke during the test. In fact, only three AI models finished with more than their starting capital. A simple rule-based heuristic containing no AI at all beat nearly all of the tested models. While AI is improving at narrow tasks like fixing bugs or following service policies, it fails at long chains of decisions under uncertainty. The researchers contrast this current failure with historical strategic steering, noting how Steve Jobs saved Apple from bankruptcy in 1997 by using a simple grid to prioritize four specific product quadrants. This suggests that the ability to allocate limited resources and adapt to changing conditions remains out of reach for today's agents.

China is rapidly closing the gap with the United States in artificial intelligence capabilities. Zhipu AI, known as Z.ai, has released its open-weight GLM-5.2 model. Some researchers claim this new model matches Mythos when it comes to cybersecurity and finding software bugs. While GLM still lags behind models from OpenAI and Anthropic on more general tasks, this specific advancement in security is a point of tension. The U.S. government has actively worked to restrict China's access to the hardware needed for training and running high-end AI. Washington has also sought to block access to powerful American models like Fable and Mythos. The Trump administration views these capabilities as critical, making the rise of GLM-5.2 a significant development in the geopolitical race for technological dominance.

Suno is attempting to move beyond its reputation as a tool for generating AI music to become a legitimate streaming destination. To achieve this, the company has launched Spark, an incubator program designed to discover and break new independent artists. Unsigned singers, songwriters, and producers can apply for the program to receive grants, mentorship, and marketing support. However, the application requires participants to agree to terms that have caused concern among users on the Suno subreddit. Specifically, artists must make their songs available on the platform for remixing. This requirement is paired with a broad license granted to Suno over the artists' works. While the program offers financial and professional resources, it creates a tension between the support provided to creators and the legal control the company gains over their intellectual property.
After the headlines, back to the weather.
This afternoon will be cloudy with temperatures between 22 and 26 degrees.
And from the weather, a quick look at the calendar.
On your calendar today: Your personal calendar has the BLISS Reading Group at six forty-five in the evening, followed by Twerchhau at seven.
Mail worth checking: Martin Genzel updated the research seminar invitations. Also, Amazon says your hair dryer is out for delivery and should arrive today between 11 and 3.
And that's it for your personal morning broadcast.
[mature adult voice, intensely emotional, tender, radiant; hold a long breath after Rise] Rise... and shine.
[very very long pause before speaking; then mature adult voice, most emotional, strong, warm, radiant, deeply encouraging; hold a long breath after Rise] Rise... and shine.
